issues.description: Claim: 'College Board Unit Six, Gene Expression and Regulation, which is twelve to sixteen percent of the AP exam.' The reference confirms Unit 6 covers DNA structure/replication (header line 367) but the '12–16%' exam-weight figure is NOT in the reference excerpt. Unsupported numeric claim.
Claim: 'about six feet long — two meters — packed into a nucleus.' Not in the reference. Unsupported.
Claim: 'about one error per a billion bases' attributed to copy fidelity. The reference supports '~1 error per 10^9 bases after proofreading' (line 408) — this matches. SUPPORTED.

Claim: 'starts in nineteen fifty-three, with Watson, Crick, and a critical X-ray image taken by Rosalind Franklin called Photo Fifty-One.' Reference confirms 'Watson & Crick, 1953; Franklin's X-ray data' (line 369). The specific name 'Photo 51' is NOT in the reference. Unsupported specific name.

Claim: 'Chargaff noticed that in any DNA sample, percent A equals percent T, and percent G equals percent C.' Reference supports %A=%T and %G=%C and attributes it to 'Chargaff's Rules' (lines 377–380). The personal name 'Chargaff' appears in the reference as a rule name; SUPPORTED.
Claim: 'In nineteen fifty-eight, Meselson and Stahl settled it.' Reference confirms 'Meselson & Stahl (1958)' (line 384). SUPPORTED.

Claim: 'grew bacteria in heavy nitrogen, switched them to light nitrogen, and then measured the density of the DNA after each round of replication.' Reference confirms the conclusion (semiconservative) but the experimental protocol details (heavy/light nitrogen, density measurement) are NOT in the reference excerpt. Unsupported procedural detail.

Claim: 'Helicase unwinds the double helix by breaking the hydrogen bonds.' Reference says 'Helicase unwinds the double helix' (line 390). SUPPORTED. The hydrogen-bond mechanism detail is not in the reference but is implicit.
Claim about SSBPs: 'coat the separated strands so they don't snap back together.' Reference: 'Single-strand binding proteins (SSBPs) keep strands apart' (line 391). SUPPORTED.

Claim about topoisomerase: 'works ahead of the fork — it cuts and rejoins the DNA to relieve the supercoiling.' Reference: 'Topoisomerase relieves supercoiling ahead of the fork' (line 392). SUPPORTED.

Claim: primase 'lays down a short RNA primer — about ten nucleotides.' Reference confirms primase lays an RNA primer (line 393–394). The specific length '~10 nucleotides' is NOT in the reference. Unsupported numeric detail.

Claim: 'DNA polymerase three takes over and adds nucleotides one at a time, always five-prime to three-prime.' Reference confirms 'DNA polymerase III (in bacteria) ... add nucleotides 5'→3'' (line 395–396). SUPPORTED.
Claim: 'short pieces called Okazaki fragments.' Reference confirms 'Okazaki fragments' (line 399). SUPPORTED.

Claim: 'Polymerase one later removes those primers, and ligase seals the nicks.' Reference confirms 'DNA polymerase I removes RNA primers and replaces with DNA' (line 400–401) and 'DNA ligase seals nicks' (line 402–403). SUPPORTED.
Claim: Okazaki fragments are 'about a thousand to two thousand bases long in bacteria.' This SPECIFIC NUMERIC RANGE is NOT in the reference excerpt. Unsupported number.
Claim: ligase 'forms the final phosphodiester bond.' Reference: 'DNA ligase seals nicks between Okazaki fragments by forming the final phosphodiester bond' (line 402–403). SUPPORTED.

Claim: 'DNA polymerase has built-in three-prime to five-prime exonuclease proofreading.' Reference: 'DNA polymerases have 3'→5' exonuclease proofreading activity (~1 error per 10^9 bases after proofreading)' (line 407–408). SUPPORTED.
Claim: telomere repeating sequence is 'T T A G G G in humans.' Reference: 'repetitive sequences (TTAGGG in humans)' (line 415). SUPPORTED.
Claim: 'Telomerase, an enzyme that carries its own RNA template, can extend telomeres.' Reference: 'Telomerase ... extends telomeres using an RNA template' (line 417–419). SUPPORTED.

Claim: 'in your body only germ cells and stem cells make active telomerase. Most cancers reactivate it.' Reference: 'Telomerase (active in germ cells, stem cells, most cancers)' (line 417–418). SUPPORTED.

Claim: 'which is part of what makes them immortal.' The word 'immortal' / interpretive framing about cancer is NOT in the reference. Unsupported interpretive claim, though the underlying fact (cancers reactivate telomerase) is supported.

Claims: 'Read OpenStax Biology, Chapter Fourteen' and 'Khan Academy AP Biology Unit Six problem sets.' These external resource references are NOT in the reference excerpt. Unsupported but acceptable as study advice rather than factual claim.

summary: Traced approximately 22 distinct factual claims across the script. 12 are directly supported by the reference excerpt (lines 367–420); 10 contain specific numbers, names, or details (12–16% exam weight, 6 ft / 2 m DNA length, Photo 51, heavy/light nitrogen protocol, ~10 nt primer length, 1000–2000 nt Okazaki fragments, 'immortal' framing, OpenStax Ch.14, Khan Academy) that are NOT in the reference excerpt. Most unsupported claims are widely accepted textbook facts but are still outside what this specific reference can verify. Two are flagged as critical per the verdict rules (specific numeric claims not in reference): the '1000–2000 base' Okazaki fragment range and the '12–16% of AP exam' weighting.
